rows.v: Scored when the puck fully crosses the goal line into the net. The fundamental unit of scoring — most games are decided by just a few.
He snapped a wrist shot top corner for the game-winning goal with seconds left.
From the old sense of 'goal' as a boundary or finish line. In hockey the puck must wholly cross the line between the posts and under the bar.
Credit given to the one or two players who passed or set up a goal-scorer. Assists count as points alongside goals in a player's stats.
She threaded a perfect pass for the assist, setting up her linemate's easy tap-in.
From 'assist' (to help). Hockey uniquely rewards up to two helpers per goal, valuing playmaking nearly as much as finishing.
Scoring three goals in a single game by one player. A celebrated feat, traditionally honored by fans throwing hats onto the ice.
After his third goal, hats rained down from the stands to salute the hat trick.
Borrowed from cricket, where a bowler taking three wickets earned a new hat. The hat-throwing tradition cemented the term in hockey lore.
When a team has more skaters than the opponent because of a penalty, giving them a temporary scoring advantage. A pivotal momentum swing.
Up a man on the power play, they cycled the puck until a lane opened for the shot.
Descriptive: the team 'plays' with a 'power' advantage in numbers. Converting power plays is often the difference between winning and losing.
When a team is short-handed due to a penalty and must defend without the puck advantage, trying to 'kill' the time until full strength returns.
Their penalty kill held firm, blocking shots and clearing the puck until the penalty expired.
Descriptive: the short-handed team works to 'kill off' the penalty time without conceding. A great PK can swing momentum back to the defending side.
When an attacker gets behind the defense with a clear path to the goalie and no defenders to beat. A thrilling one-on-one scoring chance.
He sprang a breakaway, racing in alone and deking the goalie for a highlight-reel goal.
Descriptive: the player 'breaks away' free of the defense. The pure one-on-one duel with the goalie is one of hockey's most exciting moments.
Shooting the puck immediately upon receiving a pass, without stopping it first. A quick, powerful play that catches goalies out of position.
She blasted a one-timer off the cross-ice feed before the goalie could slide over.
Descriptive: the puck is struck in 'one' motion the moment it arrives. Timing it perfectly produces one of the hardest, fastest shots in the game.
A goal scored into an open net after a trailing team has pulled its goalie for an extra attacker. The clinching goal that seals a win.
With the goalie pulled, she fired the puck the length of the ice for an empty-netter.
Descriptive: the net is 'empty' of a goalie. A team losing late pulls its goalie to attack, risking exactly this kind of game-sealing goal.
The central forward who plays the middle of the ice, takes face-offs, and links offense and defense. Often a team's playmaking hub.
As center, he won the face-off cleanly and immediately set the offense in motion.
Descriptive: this forward patrols the 'center' of the ice. Centers are typically the most all-around players, strong at both ends and in the dot.
A forward who plays along the sides ('wings') of the rink, supporting the center. Left and right wingers focus on scoring and forechecking.
The winger hugged the boards, waiting for the breakout pass to spring up the wing.
From the 'wings' (sides) of the playing area, like wings of a building or bird. Wingers patrol the outer lanes, finishing plays the center creates.
A player whose primary job is stopping the opposing offense and protecting their own goal, while also starting attacks from the back.
The defenseman stepped up at the blue line to break up the rush before it started.
Descriptive: this player's role is 'defense'. Working in pairs, they're the last line before the goalie and increasingly contribute to offense too.
Goaltender — the heavily padded player who guards the net and stops shots. The last line of defense and often a game's most important player.
The goalie sprawled across the crease to rob the shooter with a glove save.
From 'goal' + 'tender' (one who tends or guards). The goaltender's unique equipment and role make them unlike any other player on the ice.
A tough, physical player whose role is to protect teammates, intimidate opponents, and respond to rough play — sometimes by fighting.
The enforcer answered the cheap shot on the star, dropping the gloves to defend his teammate.
From 'enforce' — they 'enforce' an unwritten code of respect and retaliation. A traditional, now-fading role unique to hockey's physical culture.
A unit of three forwards (and often a defensive pairing) who play together as a coordinated group, rotating on and off the ice in shifts.
The top line clicked all night, the three forwards reading each other perfectly.
Descriptive: the forwards line up and play together as a set. Teams roll several lines through short, high-intensity shifts to stay fresh.
The team leader, identified by a 'C' on the jersey, who represents the players, communicates with officials, and sets the tone on and off the ice.
The captain rallied the bench during the intermission, demanding a stronger third period.
From the military/nautical sense of a leader. Hockey formalizes it with the 'C' worn on the jersey; assistant captains wear an 'A'.
A violation called when an attacking player crosses into the offensive zone ahead of the puck. Stops the rush and forces a face-off.
The linesman waved off the goal because the winger was offside, entering the zone too early.
From the broad sports concept of being on the wrong 'side' of the play. In hockey, players can't precede the puck over the blue line into the attacking zone.
A violation called when a player shoots the puck from behind center ice all the way across the opponent's goal line untouched. Stops play to prevent stalling.
Under pressure, the defender flung the puck down the ice, drawing an icing call.
From the old sense of shooting the puck the length of the 'ice'. The rule prevents teams from endlessly clearing the puck to waste time.
A punishment for breaking the rules, sending the offender to the penalty box and leaving their team short-handed for two or more minutes.
His trip drew a two-minute penalty, putting his team down a skater.
From 'penalty' (a punishment). Hockey's penalties remove the offender from play, directly creating the power-play and penalty-kill situations.
A penalty for hitting an opponent with the stick carried above shoulder or waist height. A common, dangerous infraction.
She caught an edge of the stick to the face, and the ref signaled high-sticking.
Descriptive: the stick is carried illegally 'high'. The rule protects players from the serious facial and head injuries an errant high stick can cause.
The method of starting or restarting play, where an official drops the puck between two opposing players who battle to win possession.
He crouched low at the face-off, winning the draw cleanly back to his defenseman.
Descriptive: two players 'face off' against each other. Every period and stoppage restarts with a face-off, making the skill of winning draws valuable.
Using the body or stick to legally separate an opponent from the puck. 'Body checking' and 'poke checking' are core defensive skills.
He laid a clean body check along the boards, knocking the puck carrier off the play.
From 'check' (to stop or restrain). Legal checking is fundamental to hockey's physical defense, though illegal checks (from behind, to the head) draw penalties.
When an official signals a penalty but lets play continue until the offending team touches the puck — letting the other team attack with an extra skater first.
Seeing the delayed penalty signal, they pulled the goalie for a six-on-five attack.
Descriptive: the penalty is 'delayed' so the non-offending team isn't disadvantaged by an immediate whistle, letting them press their momentary advantage.
Either of the two blue lines dividing the rink into zones. Crossing them governs offside and marks the boundary of the offensive and defensive zones.
He held the puck at the blue line, waiting for his teammates to clear the zone before entering.
Named simply for its color on the ice. The blue lines define the three zones and are central to the offside rule that shapes how attacks develop.
The marked area directly in front of the goal, reserved for the goalie. Attackers interfere with the goalie here at their peril.
The goal was disallowed because an attacker was standing in the crease, interfering with the goalie.
From the sense of a marked line or fold (a 'crease'). It's the goaltender's protected zone, and goals can be waved off for crease violations.
The central area of the rink between the two blue lines, belonging to neither team's offense or defense. The transition space between zones.
They clogged the neutral zone with defenders, strangling the opponent's rush before it built speed.
Descriptive: the 'neutral' middle ground between attacking zones. Controlling it ('the neutral-zone trap') is a classic defensive strategy.
The wooden and glass walls surrounding the rink that keep the puck in play. Much of hockey's physical battle happens along the boards.
The two wingers crashed into the boards, fighting for the loose puck in the corner.
From the literal wooden 'boards' (now reinforced with glass) ringing the ice. They keep play continuous and are a constant arena for physical contests.
The long implement each player uses to handle, pass, and shoot the puck. The goalie's stick has a distinct, wider blade.
He flexed his stick into the ice, loading up the slap shot.
From the everyday 'stick'. The modern composite hockey stick, with its curved blade, is the essential tool for every skill in the game.
The hard, frozen rubber disc that players shoot into the net to score. Small, fast, and frozen before games to reduce bouncing.
The puck rocketed off his stick at nearly a hundred miles an hour.
Possibly from the Irish 'poc' (to poke or strike) or the mischievous sprite Puck. The vulcanized rubber disc is frozen before play to keep it from bouncing.
The machine that resurfaces the ice between periods, shaving and smoothing it and laying down fresh water. A beloved fixture of the hockey experience.
Fans watched the Zamboni circle the rink, leaving a gleaming sheet of fresh ice behind.
A brand name, from inventor Frank Zamboni, who built the first ice-resurfacing machine in 1949. Like 'Kleenex', the name became the generic term.
Slang for the puck. 'Biscuit in the basket' is a colorful way to describe scoring a goal.
He put the biscuit in the basket on a beautiful backhand.
Affectionate hockey slang likening the round, flat puck to a biscuit. 'Biscuit in the basket' is among the sport's most cherished bits of color commentary.
The gap between a goalie's legs. Scoring 'through the five-hole' means slipping the puck between the pads.
She read the goalie dropping early and slid it through the five-hole.
From the numbering of a goalie's save zones — the four corners are holes one through four, and the gap between the legs is the fifth 'hole'.
Slang for an assist. A player with lots of 'apples' is a gifted passer and playmaker.
He didn't score but racked up three apples, setting up every goal.
Old hockey slang of uncertain origin for an assist — possibly rhyming or simply a colorful nickname that stuck in the sport's rich vocabulary.
Slang for the penalty box, where penalized players must sit and serve their time. A wry name for the box of shame.
He slammed the door of the sin bin after his careless penalty.
A playful nickname casting the penalty box as a place to atone for one's on-ice 'sins' — a beloved bit of hockey humor.
A fake or feint used to trick a defender or goalie out of position. To 'deke' someone is to outwit them with a deceptive move.
He deked left, then cut right, leaving the goalie sprawling the wrong way.
A clipping of 'decoy'. The art of the deke — selling a fake move to create an opening — is one of hockey's most exciting individual skills.
Slang for a goal celebration. A big 'celly' is an exuberant, theatrical reaction after scoring.
His over-the-top celly — sliding across the ice on his knees — fired up the whole bench.
A casual shortening of 'celebration'. The creativity and exuberance of cellys have become a celebrated, personality-filled part of modern hockey culture.
